---
title: "Planning"
format: html
---

# Title Slide

Everything, Everwhere, All At Once - open-source automation for enhanced situational awareness at a Shared Resource Laboratory

Authors, UMGCCCC logo, ISAC recognized SRL, Date

# Slide 1

Working at a small SRL, on a good day, your workflow is routine. You show up, you change the sheathe and waste, you turn on your instruments, you get them through flushing out the fluidics, you run QC, you finally get time for your second cup of coffee. The rest of the day is split between sorts, training new users, consulting on panels, bizarre experimental results, etc. 

# Slide 2

That is on a good day. But as we all know, other days, our Cytometers choose violence. Everything that can go wrong, does, and you are left fighting for dear life to get everything back into a stable enough place that. 

# Slide 3

Since we are in Florida, lets take this analogy to the beach. Most days, you are threading water. On those days, you are being battered by the waves that are intent on drowning you. 

# Slide 4

These have a cost. Your instrument is down, requiring time to schedule preventative maintenance, adding to staff burden

The instrument is down, so not earning its keep, which is about to also get expensive

The instrument is down, your users plans get disrupted and pushed back. Repeatedly over time. 

The instrument is failing, poorer data outcomes, loss of precious samples, resources, and need to repeat. More consults trying to make sense of the data, more of our time with our families going out the window. Only so many hours in the day.

And are harder to define cost. We know of examples where reputation takes hit, hard to earn back the trust after lost.

# Slide 5

We have a tendency to treat these moments as "Acts de force majeur". But the waves that drown you don't materialize out of nowhere. Instruments in laser decline leave signs on your acquired data. Gunk build up in the fluidic lines does as well. damaged qc beads have changes in MFI and spread. tandems that break down have unique signature variants. There is invaluable data already being acquired on our instruments that if we could only leverage could give us a heads up

# Slide 6

And while manufactureres and vendors have different warning systems, not end all be all. Early warnings sound nice, but what is the catch, and what is the price, because God knows your admin is going to want a better justification for that kind of expense. But what if, you never had to ask them or shell out in the first place. What if you just did it. Not all at once, but piecemeal, separate fixes for separate instruments

# Slide 6 

This is our journey at the UMGCCC FCSR, on our quest to try to implement systems in place to provide us some warning in advance, to make these hell in a handbasket days less frequent. 

# Slide 7

- How are we doing it?
- Free and open source, R, Python and Rust. 
- Exisiting and repurposed computational resources from Windows 10 
- automation/CI infrastructure. 






# Slide 3
- Usage over time plots, instrument and users. hard metrics
- Automatically updating, Warnings cancellations/alterations
- Instrument log parsing for minutes

# Slide 4
- While parsing logs, might as well parse .expt files too, monitor computer space
- Database of markers and fluors, who needs XYZ antibody?
- Pass through hotspot calculator, changes panels over time
- Parse the reference controls for Luciernaga for AF
- Parse for tandem breakdown

# Slide 5 
- Fluidics error logs enhanced QC
- Bead degradatation
- Cytometer errors
- QC
- Sorter metrics as well, nozzles
- Sterility tracking
- CytometryQC
- In progress - temperature monitors

# Slide 6
- Extended awareness, proactive defense, offense
- tailored piece meal
- still upkeep, iteration improvement, interventions
- value added
- sharing our existing ccode, not all needed, but not starting from scratch
- CytometryQC
- CytometryInR
